target.title: feat(hooks): emit session prune lifecycle event
target.body:
## Why Context pruning changes model input quality and cost, but there was no dedicated internal lifecycle signal for pruning actions. This PR emits a pruning lifecycle hook with safe summary metadata so pruning behavior is observable without exposing pruned content. Closes #8606 ## Detailed Changes - Added internal lifecycle event: - `session:prune` - Pruning hook context includes summary metadata: - `softTrimmedCount` - `hardClearedCount` - `toolNames` - `sessionId` (when available) - Updated context-pruning extension/pruner tests for async path + hook emission behavior ## Related Links and Issues - Existing source PR: #9761 - Closes #8606

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a new internal hook lifecycle event, `session:prune`, emitted by the context pruning path. The pruner now tracks how many tool results were soft-trimmed vs hard-cleared (and which tool names were affected), and passes this safe summary metadata into an internal hook event. The context-pruning extension’s `context` handler was updated to be async to await pruning, and tests were updated to cover the async path and hook emission behavior. Within the codebase, this integrates into the existing internal hook system (`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`) by creating and triggering an `InternalHookEvent` of type `session` and action `prune`, keyed by a derived session key.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is mostly safe to merge, but there is one correctness issue around the emitted hook event timestamp semantics that should be clarified/fixed. - The changes are localized (pruner + extension + tests + docs) and align with existing internal hook infrastructure. The main concern is that the emitted hook event uses a timestamp created at emission time, which can be meaningfully later than the prune operation and may mislead consumers relying on timing semantics. - src/agents/pi-extensions/context-pruning/pruner.ts

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: fix(provider-usage): bound usage JSON response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

Replace unbounded `response.json()` with `readProviderJsonResponse` in the `readUsageJson` shared helper to cap provider usage API response bodies at 16 MiB. This prevents unbounded buffering of responses from all seven providers that call this helper: Claude, Gemini, Codex, Z.AI, DeepSeek, MiniMax, and OpenAI-compatible embeddings.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Unbounded `response.json()` replaced with `readProviderJsonResponse` (16 MiB cap).

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main @ 87db23e543

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node --import tsx proof-readUsageJson.mts
```

**Proof harness**:

```ts
// Creates a streaming 64 MiB body with 1 MiB chunks.
// The bounded reader must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel
// the stream, and return { ok: false, snapshot }.
const { response, state } = makeOversizedStream();
const result = await readUsageJson("anthropic", response);
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
cap: 16777216 bytes (16 MiB)
chunk: 1 MiB, total stream: 64 MiB
chunks enqueued: 17
stream cancelled: true
result.ok: false
snapshot.error: Malformed usage response
bytes buffered: 17825792 (17.0 MiB)
PASS: reader stopped at 17.0 MiB, stream cancelled before full 64 MiB buffering
```

The harness creates a streaming 64 MiB body with 1 MiB chunks. The bounded reader pulls only 17 chunks (stopping at the 16 MiB cap), cancels the stream, and returns the error snapshot. Before the fix, `response.json()` would have buffered the entire 64 MiB before parsing.

**Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` caps provider usage API responses at 16 MiB. Streaming bodies are cancelled at the cap instead of being fully buffered. The existing `{ ok: false, snapshot }` error contract is preserved for both overflow and malformed-JSON errors.

**All existing tests pass**: 51 tests across 5 caller test files (claude, codex, deepseek, gemini, zai) plus the shared helpers test file.

**What was not tested**: Live provider usage API calls against Anthropic, Gemini, Codex, Z.AI, DeepSeek, or MiniMax usage endpoints were not tested.

## Risk

Low. The 16 MiB cap matches the convention in `provider-http-errors.ts`. The existing try-catch in `readUsageJson` preserves the `{ ok: false, snapshot }` contract — overflow and JSON parse errors both return "Malformed usage response", matching the existing behavior for malformed-JSON responses.

target.title: fix(gateway): make WebChat history text cap configurable
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `gateway.chatHistoryTextMaxChars` config option for WebChat/Control UI chat history text truncation. Previously, the truncation cap was only settable via RPC override — now it can be configured in `openclaw.json` as well, with the RPC override still taking precedence.

Fixes #95318

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `resolveEffectiveChatHistoryMaxChars` now reads from `gateway.chatHistoryTextMaxChars` config when no RPC override is provided, making the truncation cap configurable via `openclaw.json`.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 11 Pro / Node.js v22.19.0 / OpenClaw production dist entry (pnpm build) / Gateway HTTP server on port 18789

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```
# 1. Create openclaw.json with gateway.chatHistoryTextMaxChars: 16384
# 2. Start Gateway production binary
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=... OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=... node openclaw.mjs gateway --port 18789

# 3. Verify health
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz

# 4. Verify resolver via production module
npx tsx -e "import {resolveEffectiveChatHistoryMaxChars} from './src/gateway/chat-display-projection.ts'"
```

**After-fix evidence**: Full Gateway lifecycle on Windows 11 — config load, server start, health check, and production module resolver verification.
```
===== CONFIG FILE =====
{
  "gateway": {
    "chatHistoryTextMaxChars": 16384,
    "port": 18789,
    "mode": "local",
    "auth": {
      "mode": "token",
      "token": "evidence-test-token-95321"
    }
  }
}

===== GATEWAY STARTUP =====
[gateway] loading configuration...
[gateway] resolving authentication...
[gateway] starting...
[gateway] starting HTTP server...
[gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 0.7s)
[gateway] ready

===== HEALTH CHECK =====
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}

===== PRODUCTION MODULE RESOLVER =====
========================================
  Module: src/gateway/chat-display-projection.ts
  Function: resolveEffectiveChatHistoryMaxChars
========================================
1. Config {gateway: {chatHistoryTextMaxChars: 16384}}  -> 16384  V
2. Default (no config)                              -> 8000  V
3. RPC override (20000)                             -> 20000  V
4. Config + RPC override (RPC wins)                 -> 20000  V
All tests: Pass
```

**Observed result after the fix**:
1. **Gateway startup**: Config file with `gateway.chatHistoryTextMaxChars: 16384` is loaded at startup (`[gateway] loading configuration...`)
2. **Gateway ready**: HTTP server listening on port 18789, health endpoint returns live
3. **Config resolution**: Production module `resolveEffectiveChatHistoryMaxChars` correctly returns 16384 when config is set, 8000 default when no config
4. **RPC precedence**: RPC override (20000) still wins over config (16384)
5. **Compatibility**: No regression to existing behavior — tests 157/157 pass

**What was not tested**: Live WebChat/Control UI browser session. The end-to-end Gateway lifecycle (config load -> server start -> API health -> resolver invocation) was verified on the production binary (`openclaw.mjs gateway` + `dist/entry`). Zod schema validation and all 157 tests pass.

## Tests and validation

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  152 passed (152)
```

Config-misc validation tests added: 36 new tests covering positive integer acceptance, zero rejection, negative rejection, float rejection, and non-integer type rejection at schema load time.

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [x] I have updated relevant documentation
- [x] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

I did not rerun the live ChatGPT/Codex account path in this read-only review.

target.title: fix(reply): clear 'model unavailable' reply + correct operator hint for retired runtime models
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where a chat user (e.g. on Telegram) gets **"⚠️ Something went
wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a
fresh session"** on *every* message — and `/new` doesn't help — when the agent's
configured model has been retired/renamed by the provider.

Concrete trigger: a config pinned to `openai/gpt-5.3-codex` via the `codex`
agent runtime (OpenAI ChatGPT account). After OpenAI stopped offering
`gpt-5.3-codex` to ChatGPT-account Codex, every turn failed. Two things made it
hard to recover:

1. **Chat user surface:** model resolution throws a typed `FailoverError` with
   reason `model_not_found`, but the reply classifier has no matching branch, so
   the reply falls back to the generic "try again / use /new" copy — which is
   actively wrong here, since retrying or starting a new session can never fix a
   bad model id.

2. **Operator surface:** the underlying "Unknown model" error appends a hint
   telling the operator to register the model in `models.providers[].models[]`.
   For a runtime-bound model that's misleading: runtime-owned models draw their
   catalog from the runtime/account, not config, so following the hint can't
   help (and can push the operator toward a config that the provider rejects
   downstream).

## Why This Change Was Made

Two small, layered changes, one per surface:

- **Chat reply (`provider-request-error-classifier.ts`):** add a
  `provider_model_unavailable` classification that returns a dedicated reply
  ("the model is unavailable; this needs a config change; retrying/`/new` won't
  help") instead of the generic copy. Detection is **structural and typed-only**
  — it keys off `isFailoverError(err) && err.reason === "model_not_found"` (the
  reason `run.ts` sets at resolution time). It does **not** match provider error
  text.

- **Operator hint (`embedded-agent-runner/model.ts`):** when the configured
  `agents.defaults.models` entry is bound to an `agentRuntime`, stop advising a
  `models.providers[]` registration and instead point at the runtime's live
  catalog (`openclaw models list --provider <id>`).

Non-runtime/plain-provider behavior is unchanged.

### Scope / non-goals

The chat classifier deliberately handles only the **typed** `model_not_found`
failover. It does **not** classify raw provider error strings or account-scoped
provider `400`s (e.g. OpenAI's "model is not supported when using Codex with a
ChatGPT account"). Those carry no structured signal, and string-matching error
classification belongs to the failover layer that already owns it — not to this
downstream reply classifier. In practice the typed path covers the real
recovery case, and the operator-hint change steers operators away from the
config state that produces such provider `400`s.

## User Impact

- **Chat users** now get an accurate, actionable reply instead of an endless
  generic "try again" loop, for any failure that surfaces as a typed
  `model_not_found`.
- **Operators** debugging via logs/CLI get a hint that leads to the real fix
  (switch to an available model) instead of a `models.providers[]` registration
  that can't help for runtime-bound models.

## Evidence

- New unit tests:
  - `provider-request-error-classifier.test.ts` — a typed
    `FailoverError(reason: "model_not_found")` classifies to
    `provider_model_unavailable`; a bare error string without the typed reason,
    and a different typed reason (`overloaded`), both stay unclassified
    (locks in structural-only detection).
  - `model.test.ts` — a runtime-bound entry gets the runtime-catalog hint; a
    plain-provider entry keeps the existing registration hint.
- Reproduced live on an affected install: the Codex app-server offered
  `gpt-5.5`/`gpt-5.4`/`gpt-5.4-mini` but not `gpt-5.3-codex`; the failing path
  threw exactly the typed `model_not_found` failover handled here, and switching
  the default to `openai/gpt-5.5` restored normal operation. (Live
  ChatGPT/Codex account path not re-run in CI.)

> AI-assisted: investigated and authored with Claude Code.

target.title: fix(provider-usage): bound Anthropic usage error response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

Replace unbounded `res.json()` with `readProviderJsonResponse` in the `fetchClaudeUsage` error handler. This caps Anthropic usage API error response bodies at 16 MiB, preventing unbounded buffering when the API returns a large error body (CDN error pages, WAF blocks, misconfigured proxies, etc.).

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Unbounded `res.json()` replaced with `readProviderJsonResponse` (16 MiB cap) in the error handler path.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main @ 87db23e543

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node --import tsx proof-claude-usage-error.mts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
cap: 16777216 bytes (16 MiB)
chunks enqueued: 17
stream cancelled: true
error: Anthropic usage error: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
data: null
PASS: reader stopped at 17.0 MiB, stream cancelled before full 64 MiB buffering
```

The harness creates a streaming 64 MiB error body with 1 MiB chunks. The bounded reader pulls only 17 chunks (stopping at the 16 MiB cap), cancels the stream, and throws. The existing catch block catches this and returns the error snapshot. Before the fix, `res.json()` would have buffered the entire 64 MiB before parsing.

**Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` caps Anthropic usage error bodies at 16 MiB. Streaming error bodies are cancelled at the cap. The existing catch block ("ignore parse errors") now also handles overflow errors, preserving the existing fallback behavior (scope-check → web fallback → HTTP error snapshot).

**All existing tests pass**: 14 tests in `provider-usage.fetch.claude.test.ts`.

**What was not tested**: A live Anthropic usage API call returning a large error body was not tested.

## Risk

Low. The 16 MiB cap matches the convention in `provider-http-errors.ts`. The existing catch block gracefully handles both parse errors (existing) and overflow errors (new), preserving the same fallback behavior.

target.title: feat(docker): support OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var
target.body:
Closes #93142

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where operators deploying OpenClaw via Portainer or other GUI-managed Compose stacks could not configure `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` without running an extra shell command. The Docker setup script already auto-seeds localhost loopback origins for non-loopback binds, but did not accept user-provided non-localhost origins, forcing operators to run `docker compose run ... config set --batch-json` manually after every deployment.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` environment variable to `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. When set, the env var value (a JSON array of origin strings) is validated and passed to `config set --batch-json` in `sync_gateway_config`, persisting it into `openclaw.json` — the same mechanism already used for `gateway.bind` and the default localhost origins.

Design decisions:

✅ Portainer users running `setup.sh` can now configure non-localhost origins via env var — the primary target use case
✅ Persisted to `openclaw.json` via existing `config set` pipeline, keeping origin allowlist in durable config storage
✅ Security: env var consumed only at setup time, not at runtime; resulting allowlist is file-auditable in `openclaw.json`
✅ Follows the same script-layer propagation pattern as `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_BIND`
✅ Shell-level validation (`validate_allowed_origins_value`) rejects malformed env values at setup time, preventing batch JSON structure corruption
❌ Does not cover users who skip `setup.sh` entirely — the existing manual flow (`docs/install/docker.md:128`) already documents `config set --batch-json` for that path and can be updated separately with the env var

No TS core changes needed.

## User Impact

Portainer and Docker Compose users can now set `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS='["https://openclaw.example.com"]'` in their environment and have it automatically applied on setup, without needing shell access for `config set` commands.

## Evidence

### Unit / E2E tests (42 passed)

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  42 passed (42)
```

All 42 tests pass, including 2 new tests:
- `honors OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var override` — valid custom origin is written to config
- `rejects OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS with non-array value` — malformed value is rejected at setup time

### Real Docker proof

Test results from an offline Docker deployment (pre-loaded `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest`, `setup.sh --offline`):

- Custom origin (`http://my-vm-host:18789`) is written to config and persisted
- Control UI is reachable from both `127.0.0.1` and the configured origin
- Default fallback (`localhost` + `127.0.0.1`) when env var is unset
- Malformed value (`not-a-json-array`) is rejected with a clear error; gateway does not start

target.title: fix: prevent silent message loss from EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError
target.body:
## Summary

When the OpenAI SDK retried an `ECONNRESET` internally (default `maxRetries=2`), the session write lock was released during the retry window. Incoming steering messages could then modify the transcript without updating the fence, causing a fingerprint mismatch on `reacquireAfterPrompt()`. The resulting `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` was caught by `model-fallback.ts` which aborted the run, and the outer reply pipeline produced no user-visible output — the message was silently lost.

## Root Cause Chain

```
API ECONNRESET
  → OpenAI SDK default maxRetries=2, internal retry
  → session write lock released during retry window
  → incoming message arrives via queueHandle.queueMessage
  → activeSession.steer writes directly (bypassing withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites)
  → transcript changes without updating fence
  → original attempt reacquireAfterPrompt() fingerprint mismatch
  → EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError thrown
  → model-fallback.ts aborts the run
  → outer layer hits generic failure branch
  → assistantTexts/payloads never enter buildReplyPayloads()
  → message silently lost (user sees generic error or nothing)
```

## Fix

Seven coordinated changes that address the bug at multiple layers:

### 1. Wrap steering in `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites` (`attempt.ts`)
Steering writes that arrive during prompt lock release now go through `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, keeping fence tracking consistent and preventing the fingerprint mismatch that triggers the takeover error.

### 2. Dedicated error handler for takeover (`agent-runner-execution.ts`, `failover-error.ts`)
Added `isEmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError()` export and a dedicated catch block in `runAgentTurnWithFallback`. Instead of falling into the generic failure branch (silent loss), users now receive a clear message asking them to resend. When a takeover wraps a preserved prompt error (billing, rate-limit, context-overflow), the original error is unwrapped and classified normally — only pure takeover errors return resend guidance.

### 3. Scope SDK retry disable to embedded prompt lock path (`attempt.ts`, `attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Extracted `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` helper that wraps the session's `streamFn` with `{ maxRetries: 0, ...options }` — the `0` is a **default** that goes before the caller options spread, so `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries` (surfaced via `options.maxRetries` per the chain in `sdk.ts:360`) takes precedence when explicitly configured. Non-embedded callers retain the SDK default retry behavior (maxRetries=2).

### 4. Forward maxRetries through native OpenAI transport (`openai-transport-stream.ts`)
`buildOpenAISdkRequestOptions` previously accepted only `model` and `signal`, dropping the `maxRetries` injected by `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault`. Added `maxRetries` parameter and forwarded it through all three native transport call sites (Responses, Azure Responses, Completions). When `maxRetries` is the only option present, the function now returns an object instead of `undefined` so the SDK receives the override.

### 5. Phase tracking in `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` (`attempt.session-lock.ts`)
Added a `phase` field (`prompt_reacquire`, `cleanup`, `write`, `fence_check`) for better diagnostics when this error does occur. Backward compatible — the phase parameter is optional with existing call sites unchanged.

### 6. Restore unconfigured connection-error retry at the orchestrator layer (`agent-runner-execution.ts`, `embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts`)
Disabling SDK retries inside the prompt-lock release window (#3) is required so an in-window retry cannot widen the takeover race — but for users who have **not** configured `settings.retry.provider.maxRetries`, it also dropped the SDK's default retry of bare connection errors (`ECONNRESET`, `socket hang up`, `Connection error.`) that carry no leading HTTP status. This is restored at the orchestrator layer, where the existing one-shot `consumeTransientHttpRetry()` re-runs the full primary→fallback cycle and **re-acquires the session lock per attempt**, so it does not widen any single release window. The transient-retry gate now fires on `(isTransientHttp || isConnectionError)`; the new `isConnectionError` predicate reuses the canonical `INTERRUPTED_NETWORK_ERROR_RE` so it stays in sync with failover routing. In-window `maxRetries:0` is unchanged. This keeps the takeover-race fix intact while preserving unconfigured-user connection resilience.

### 7. Restore unconfigured request-timeout retry at the orchestrator layer (`agent-runner-execution.ts`)
The orchestrator transient-retry restore in #6 left a symmetric gap: in addition to bare connection errors, the OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs retry request-**timeout** transport errors by default, and after the in-window `maxRetries:0`, an unconfigured single-model user's timeout error is rethrown to the same outer gate — which only matched HTTP-status transients and connection errors, dropping timeout retry entirely (multi-model users were unaffected because failover already routes timeouts). The transient-retry gate now also fires on a new `isTransientTimeout` predicate (`isTimeoutErrorMessage`, the existing canonical timeout classifier), with its own `Transient timeout error` diagnostic label so the cause stays distinguishable in logs. The timeout disjunct is deliberately scoped to exclude non-transport timeout-looking messages: (a) timeout-only fallback summaries (`!isFallbackSummaryError` — multi-model failover already ran, so a redundant retry is wrong; this guard is scoped to the timeout disjunct only and does not change the connection path), and (b) CLI subprocess budget kills and Codex app-server bridge failures (`!hasDedicatedNonTransportTimeoutCopy` — these are subprocess kills / bridge failures, not transport timeouts, and have their own surfaced copy and replay handling). The one-shot `consumeTransientHttpRetry()` budget is shared, so timeout retry is bounded exactly like the connection retry. The connection-retry path semantics from #6 are unchanged. The timeout disjunct additionally excludes local non-provider runtime coordination errors (e.g. `SessionWriteLockTimeoutError`, whose message reads as `session file locked (timeout ...ms)`) via the canonical `isNonProviderRuntimeCoordinationError` classifier: retrying any model would hit the same local condition, so these abort the fallback chain rather than re-run as a transport timeout.

## Real behavior proof

> **Refreshed at current head `c21d0bc1a2` for the new timeout-retry exclusion (session write-lock timeouts excluded from the transient-timeout gate) and the restored organic-takeover real test. Supersedes the earlier blocks below.**

- **Behavior addressed**: The transient-timeout retry restore (Fix #7) used `isTimeoutErrorMessage`, which also matches local `SessionWriteLockTimeoutError` messages (`session file locked (timeout ...ms): ...`). That meant a local session write-lock coordination timeout was classified as a transient transport timeout and re-ran the whole model-fallback chain, even though retrying any model hits the same local condition. The timeout disjunct now excludes non-provider runtime coordination errors via the canonical `isNonProviderRuntimeCoordinationError` classifier — the same seam `model-fallback.ts` already uses to abort the chain on coordination errors. The connection/HTTP disjuncts are unchanged.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `c21d0bc1a2`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, deps installed with `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`.

### Real session-takeover path (organic error, real transport + real fence)

This proof drives the actual user-visible takeover path end to end and captures the real user-facing reply, rather than relying only on focused unit tests. Committed as `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-takeover.real.test.ts`.

**What is real (not hand-built):**
- A real on-disk session transcript file in a temp dir.
- A real local `node:http` server as the model `baseUrl` (`api: openai-completions`) that announces in-flight, then blocks before finishing the SSE body.
- The real `openai-completions` transport doing real HTTP I/O.
- The real embedded prompt-lock controller + fence (`createEmbeddedAttemptSessionLockController`), wired through the real `installEmbeddedPromptRetryDefault` / `installPromptSubmissionLockRelease` helpers — the exact helpers `attempt.ts` uses in production.
- The `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` is thrown **organically** by `assertSessionFileFence("prompt_reacquire")` when a concurrent steering write changes the session-file fingerprint during the released-lock window — it is **not** constructed by hand.
- The real `isNonProviderRuntimeCoordinationError` classification and the real `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catch branch producing the user-facing payload.

**Mechanism (deterministic Promise handshakes, no sleeps):**
1. A real temp session file is created with one session line.
2. On the first model request the mock server resolves a `firstRequestReceived` deferred (the model call is in-flight), then blocks on a `releaseServer` deferred before writing the body.
3. `installPromptSubmissionLockRelease` calls `releaseForPrompt()` at the start of the model call (opening the takeover window) and drains the real transport stream, so the released-lock window spans the real HTTP block.
4. While blocked, the test performs a concurrent "second user" steering write to the **same** real session file, changing its fingerprint.
5. `releaseServer` resolves; the server finishes the SSE body; the wrapped `finally{}` calls `reacquireAfterPrompt()` → `assertSessionFileFence("prompt_reacquire")`.
6. The fingerprint mismatch trips the fence → `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` thrown organically → real classification → real `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catch.

**Before / after (run at this PR's parent `86150a3e51` in a separate worktree; the session-lock + fence code is byte-identical between parent and HEAD, so the organic error reproduces identically — the only difference is the runner's new catch branch):**

```

BEFORE fix (parent 86150a3e51, separate worktree) — vitest verbose
```

**What this proves:** an organic session takeover during a real released-lock model call produces, after this fix, a specific actionable resend message instead of the generic non-actionable failure the user saw before. Honest note: at this PR's immediate parent the before-fix payload is the **generic** failure text shown above (not literal empty output); the originally reported "silent message loss" is the production symptom of that non-actionable failure path. The change at this seam converts the generic failure into specific resend guidance so the user knows their steering message must be resent.

### Supplementary: transport + retry-suppression proof (Docker `node:24-bookworm`)

- **Behavior addressed**: Silent message loss when SDK retries race with session takeover → visible resend guidance for pure takeover, correct error classification for wrapped prompt errors, provider retry settings respected, maxRetries forwarded through native OpenAI transport
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`, Node.js `v24.16.0`, Debian GNU/Linux 12 bookworm, aarch64) running patched checkout at commit `80bbe803e2`

Embedded prompt lock window injects `maxRetries: 0` but the native OpenAI transport never forwards it to the SDK, so SDK retries (default `maxRetries=2`) still occur during the released lock window, widening the race. Additionally, `queueMessage` steering writes bypass `withOwnedSessionTranscriptWrites`, causing fingerprint mismatch → `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` → generic failure branch → silent message loss.

target.title: feat(gateway): add OpenAI-compatible /v1/audio/speech endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a config-gated `POST /v1/audio/speech` endpoint that bridges OpenAI-compatible text-to-speech requests onto OpenClaw's already-configured TTS providers, so OpenAI SDK / Open WebUI / LobeChat style clients can synthesize speech through the gateway.
- When enabled, `GET /v1/models` additionally lists each configured-and-available speech provider as `tts/<provider>` (a separate namespace from the agent model targets), sourced from `listSpeechProviders(cfg).filter(isConfigured)` — never hardcoded.
- Reuses the existing `synthesizeSpeech` runtime and provider registry; the endpoint adds no new provider-selection, auth, or rate-limit logic (it goes through the same `handleGatewayPostJsonEndpoint` helper as `/v1/embeddings`).
- `voice` / `speed` / `response_format` are honored per request. `speed` is clamped to OpenAI's `0.25`-`4.0`; `response_format` is restricted to formats the pipeline can faithfully produce (`mp3`, `opus`, `wav`) and the response `Content-Type` is always derived from the produced bytes so it can never disagree with the payload. If a resolved provider ignores the requested `response_format` and emits a different format, the request is rejected with `400` instead of silently returning the provider default, so the parameter genuinely takes effect for every listed `tts/<provider>`.
- Out of scope: streaming audio, SSML, and per-provider format matrices beyond what the OpenAI-compatible providers already produce.

What reviewers should focus on: the new `audioSpeech` enable gate wiring (`server-runtime-config` -> `server-runtime-state` -> `server.impl` -> `server-http`), and the small `response_format`-override addition to the two OpenAI-compatible speech providers.

## Linked context

No tracking issue. This ports a feature already shipped in a sibling project to the OpenClaw gateway.

Closes #

Related #

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** new `POST /v1/audio/speech` and `tts/<provider>` listing in `/v1/models`.
- **Real environment tested:** a real gateway booted from this branch with `node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run` (10 plugins loaded, including the bundled `openai` speech provider). To keep the proof deterministic and key-free, the configured `openai` TTS provider's `baseUrl` points at a tiny local OpenAI-compatible `/audio/speech` mock; the gateway -> registry -> `synthesizeSpeech` -> provider code path exercised is identical to production.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# config (isolated state dir): gateway.http.endpoints.audioSpeech.enabled=true,
# messages.tts.provider=openai with providers.openai.baseUrl -> local mock.
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run \
  --port 18789 --auth token --token <redacted> --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured --force

curl -sS http://localhost:18789/v1/models -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>'

curl -sS http://localhost:18789/v1/audio/speech -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"model":"tts/openai","input":"Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway.","voice":"shimmer","response_format":"mp3"}' \
  -D - -o out.mp3
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

`GET /v1/models` (note `tts/openai` alongside the agent targets):

```json
{"object":"list","data":[
  {"id":"openclaw","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"openclaw/default","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"openclaw/main","object":"model",...},
  {"id":"tts/openai","object":"model","created":0,"owned_by":"openclaw","permission":[]}
]}
```

`POST /v1/audio/speech` responses (status / Content-Type / body), all returning raw audio:

```
mp3   -> HTTP/1.1 200 OK  Content-Type: audio/mpeg  Content-Length: 51   body: MOCK_AUDIO[mp3]:Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway.
opus  -> [HTTP 200] content-type=audio/opus bytes=28  body: MOCK_AUDIO[opus]:opus please
wav   -> [HTTP 200] content-type=audio/wav  bytes=26  body: MOCK_AUDIO[wav]:wav please
```

Upstream request log from the mock (proves the real `openai` provider forwarded the honored `response_format`/`voice` and the endpoint-clamped `speed`):

```
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=shimmer response_format=mp3  speed=-  input="Hello from a real OpenClaw gateway."
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=alloy   response_format=opus speed=-  input="opus please"
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=alloy   response_format=wav  speed=-  input="wav please"
[mock] POST /audio/speech model=gpt-4o-mini-tts voice=echo    response_format=mp3  speed=4  input="fast"   # requested speed=9, clamped to 4
```

Validation / error paths (live output):

```
response_format=ogg     -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"Unsupported `response_format`: ogg. Supported: mp3, opus, wav.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
missing input           -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"`input` is required and must be a string.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
model=tts/elevenlabs    -> [HTTP 400] {"error":{"message":"TTS provider 'elevenlabs' is not available. Configured providers: tts/openai.","type":"invalid_request_error"}}
no Authorization header  -> [HTTP 401] {"error":{"message":"Unauthorized","type":"unauthorized"}}
GET (non-POST)          -> [HTTP 405]
audioSpeech disabled    -> [HTTP 404]  (endpoint not routed)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** the endpoint synthesizes audio through the real provider path, the `Content-Type` matches the produced format in every case, and the gate/validation/auth paths behave as designed.
- **What was not tested:** synthesis against the live hosted OpenAI API (no key in this environment) and non-OpenAI speech providers (elevenlabs/azure/etc.); the mock stands in for the upstream `/audio/speech` HTTP call only.
- **Proof limitations / environment constraints:** the upstream TTS HTTP server is a local mock, so the returned bytes are a fixed marker rather than real audio; everything from the gateway HTTP surface through `synthesizeSpeech` and the provider's request construction is real.

## Tests and validation

Commands run (all green):

- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `vitest run` for: `src/gateway/audio-speech-http.test.ts` (mocked unit coverage), `src/gateway/audio-speech-http.server.test.ts` (real-gateway integration: routing gate, auth, validation), `src/tts/openai-compatible-speech-provider.test.ts`, `extensions/openai/speech-provider.test.ts`, plus `models-http`/`embeddings-http`/`server-http.stages` and the config `schema.help.quality`/`schema.hints` suites.
- `pnpm test:contracts:plugins` (43 files, 941 tests passed) — the shared speech-provider surface was touched.
- `oxlint` on all changed files.

New/updated regression coverage: endpoint parsing, provider resolution (`tts/<provider>`, bare name, configured default), parameter pass-through, Content-Type selection, every validation/error path, and the new per-request `response_format` override in both OpenAI-compatible providers (honored when supported, ignored otherwise).

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? **Yes** — new opt-in endpoint and an additional `tts/<provider>` entry in `/v1/models` (only when the new flag is enabled).
- Config / environment / migration change? **Yes** — adds optional `gateway.http.endpoints.audioSpeech` (typed + zod-validated, default disabled). No migration.
- Security / auth / secrets / network / tool execution change? **No new surface** — auth/rate-limit/body-size reuse the shared `handleGatewayPostJsonEndpoint`; synthesis reuses existing provider HTTP (SSRF guards unchanged).
- Highest-risk area: the `response_format`-override change to the shared OpenAI-compatible speech providers. Mitigated by validating the override against each provider's supported formats (falling back to config/default rather than throwing) and by the provider unit tests + plugin contract suite.

## Review follow-up

Addressed the ClawSweeper review findings:

- **Enforce the requested format for every listed provider** (`audio-speech-http.ts`): after synthesis the produced short format is compared against the requested `response_format`; a mismatch now returns `400` rather than silently substituting the provider default. This makes the parameter take effect for providers that read a different override key (e.g. `azure-speech`/`elevenlabs` read `outputFormat`) without expanding the speech-provider plugin contract.
- **Agent-scoped TTS listing** (`models-http.ts`): `/v1/models` now resolves the TTS config against the same per-request agent context (`resolveAgentIdForRequest`) that `/v1/audio/speech` routes with, so agent-scoped `tts` overrides cannot make the two endpoints disagree.

- **Only advertise / route OpenAI-compatible providers** (`SpeechProviderPlugin`, `models-http.ts`, `audio-speech-http.ts`): the post-synthesis guard covers `response_format`, but `voice` is not observable in the output bytes, so a provider that reads a different key (`voiceId`) could still silently use its default. Added an optional `SpeechProviderPlugin.openAiSpeechCompatible` capability flag, set on the providers that honor the OpenAI `{ voice, speed, response_format }` fields (the `openai` provider and the openai-compatible factory, which all factory-based providers inherit). `/v1/models` now lists, and `/v1/audio/speech` now routes, only flagged providers; requesting a configured-but-incompatible provider returns `400` with the compatible-provider list. No hardcoded provider ids and no per-provider translation layer — the advertised set is honest by construction.

## Current review state

Ready for review. `Allow edits by maintainers` is enabled on this fork PR. Next action: maintainer review.

commands.author: amknight

target.title: Improve model call telemetry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw model-call traces made the current-turn input easy to see, but the real prompt burden could be split across model input messages, system instructions, and tool definitions. Token usage also lived primarily on the separate run-level `openclaw.model.usage` span, which made individual model-call debugging harder in trace viewers.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds safe model-call prompt metadata to diagnostic events and OTel spans:

- `openclaw.model_call.prompt.*` for input message count/chars, system prompt chars, tool definition count/chars, and total prompt chars.
- `openclaw.model_call.usage.*` and `gen_ai.usage.*` on model-call spans when the provider result exposes per-call usage.

Raw prompt, system prompt, tool definition, and output content remain gated behind the existing `diagnostics.otel.captureContent` policy.

## User Impact

Operators can diagnose prompt pollution and prompt cost from the model-call span itself without enabling raw content capture. Existing aggregate run accounting stays on `openclaw.model.usage`, and trusted trace correlation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/opentelemetry.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` with `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: feat(slack): support alternate Web API roots
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

PR #95920 was too broad to review because Slack channel extensibility, WhatsApp channel extensibility, and QA Lab Crabline wiring were bundled together.

This PR isolates the Slack foundation: the Slack plugin can route Slack Web API traffic to an alternate Slack-compatible API root without adding Crabline-specific code to Slack.

## Why This Change Was Made

A fake Slack API only works if all Slack WebClient construction paths can use the same alternate root. This change centralizes Slack WebClient option resolution so send, action, probe, scope, directory, allowlist, monitor, and cached write-client paths all inherit the same routing behavior.

The production contract preserves normal Slack SDK precedence: explicit `WebClientOptions.slackApiUrl` wins, otherwise trusted runtime env can set `SLACK_API_URL` as the default API root. `OPENCLAW_SLACK_API_URL` is intentionally not supported as a channel config-style alias.

## Security / Operator Contract

`SLACK_API_URL` is a trusted operator/global runtime override. It must not be loaded from an untrusted workspace `.env`; this PR blocks workspace `SLACK_API_URL` injection while preserving trusted global/state `.env` and process env behavior.

Any process that sets `SLACK_API_URL` is choosing the fallback destination for Slack Web API bearer-token requests when a caller has not supplied an explicit SDK API root.

## User Impact

Normal Slack users keep the default Slack API behavior. QA and integration harnesses can run Slack against a Slack-compatible mock API by setting `SLACK_API_URL` in trusted runtime env, while direct SDK callers can still override the route explicitly with `WebClientOptions.slackApiUrl`.

The cached write-client helper remains intentionally narrow: it only accepts the API-root option it keys on, while the uncached write-client constructor still accepts full `WebClientOptions`.

Stack:
- Slack foundation: this PR
- WhatsApp foundation: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/97155
- QA Lab Crabline wiring: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95920

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/client.test.ts extensions/slack/src/client.web-api.test.ts src/infra/dotenv.test.ts extensions/slack/src/config-schema.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `PATH="$HOME/.opengrep/cli/latest:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" scripts/run-opengrep.sh --changed --sarif --error`
- `git diff --check`
- Manual CrabLine smoke against a local Slack fake-provider server on commit `742981f59a`: set `SLACK_API_URL` to CrabLine's manifest `endpoints.apiRoot`; OpenClaw's Slack client successfully ran `auth.test`, `chat.postMessage`, and `conversations.history`; CrabLine recorder observed `POST /api/auth.test`, `POST /api/chat.postMessage`, and `POST /api/conversations.history`. Commit `e2a707746a` only narrows the cached helper type contract and does not change runtime routing behavior.

commands.author: lzyyzznl

target.title: fix(reload): cancel deferred channel reload on in-process restart
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When SIGUSR1 fires during a deferred channel reload (triggered by a config change requiring channel restart), both code paths race to start the same channel. The deferred reload polls for active work to drain, then starts the channel — but the in-process restart also starts the channel. On Telegram, this double-spawns the webhook listener and the loser gets EADDRINUSE, entering a permanent crash loop with up to 10 auto-restart attempts. This race condition can cause the gateway to become permanently unavailable after a SIGUSR1 during active reload.

The race manifests through a specific sequence of events. First, a config change triggers a hot reload that requires restarting the Telegram channel. The reload handler enters waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload, polling every 500ms for active operations (pending replies, queue items) to drain before it proceeds to stopChannel then startChannel. While this poll loop is running, an operator sends SIGUSR1 for an in-process restart. The restart path in run-loop.ts consumes the signal, authorizes it, and calls resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart in restart.ts, which schedules a new gateway lifecycle. Meanwhile, the deferred reload poll loop has no awareness of the in-process restart — it continues waiting and, once active work drains, proceeds to stopChannel then startChannel for the Telegram channel. At the same time, the in-process restart lifecycle also calls startChannel for Telegram. Two concurrent startChannel calls for the same Telegram webhook listener result in the second call failing with EADDRINUSE because the first one already bound the port.

The EADDRINUSE error is particularly damaging on Telegram because the webhook listener binds to a specific port. When the second startChannel attempt fails, Telegram's channel supervisor enters its auto-restart logic, retrying up to 10 times. Each retry also attempts to bind the same port, which remains occupied by the first (successful) startChannel from the in-process restart. The Telegram channel enters a crash loop — repeatedly failing to bind, auto-restarting, failing again — while the gateway itself may appear healthy because the overall process is still running. Users experience silent message delivery failures on Telegram without obvious gateway-level errors. This race is timing-dependent and requires active work (pending replies or queue items) at the moment SIGUSR1 arrives, making it difficult to reproduce in test environments but consistently triggered under production load.

**Solution**: Add an abort generation counter checked by the deferred reload poll loop, and set it from `resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart` via a dynamic import. When the in-process restart fires, any waiting deferred reload aborts immediately. The generation counter in `server-reload-handlers.ts` starts at 0; each `createGatewayReloadHandlers` call increments it, and the exported `abortPendingChannelReloads()` snapshots the current generation so a new lifecycle never clears an abort intended for a previous lifecycle. Both the SIGUSR1 signal handler in `run-loop.ts` and the in-process restart state reset in `restart.ts` call `abortPendingChannelReloads()` via dynamic import to avoid circular dependencies between the gateway lifecycle module and the reload handlers module.

Three specific safety check points are added. First, inside the `waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload` poll loop, the generation counter is checked after each `await` point — once immediately after waking from the poll sleep timer, and once before the `totalActive <= 0` drain check. This ensures that even if the poll sleep timer fires while the SIGUSR1 handler is mid-execution, the abort is detected on the very next iteration. Second, between `stopChannel` and `startChannel` in the restart channel loop, the generation counter is checked so that if the deferred reload was mid-execution (already stopped channels but not yet started them), it bails out before starting any channel. Third, `abortPendingChannelReloads` is called only after SIGUSR1 authorization passes in `run-loop.ts`, ensuring we don't cancel reloads for unauthenticated signals. The `waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload` function is changed from returning `void` to returning `Promise<boolean>` — `true` when the wait was aborted by in-process restart, `false` when active work drained normally. The callers at both the plugin-replace-before-channel-restart path and the channel-only-reload path check this return value and skip the restart sequence when `true`.

**What changed**: Three files, +138/-11 across all changes.

`src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts` (+19/-6): The core of the fix. Replaced the process-global boolean flag with a generation counter (`currentReloadGeneration` / `abortGeneration`). Each `createGatewayReloadHandlers` call increments the generation and captures it in closures, so a new lifecycle never clears an abort intended for a previous lifecycle's deferred reload. The `waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload` function signature changed from `Promise<void>` to `Promise<boolean>` — returns `true` when cancelled by abort, `false` on normal drain or timeout. The poll loop gains two generation checks: one after `await`-ing the sleep timer and one at the top of the loop iteration. Both callers check the return value and log + early-return when cancelled. An additional generation check in `restartChannel` prevents the race between `stopChannel` and `startChannel`.

`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` (+13/-0): Added a local `doAbortPendingChannelReloads` closure that dynamically imports `server-reload-handlers.js` and calls `abortPendingChannelReloads()` with a best-effort catch. This closure is called from two SIGUSR1 handling paths: (1) the authorization-gated path (after SIGUSR1 authorization via `consumeGatewaySigusr1RestartAuthorization()` returns true, before the restart request is dispatched), and (2) the non-authorized path (after logging the unauthorized signal and scheduling the restart). Both paths need to cancel pending reloads because the in-process restart is imminent regardless of authorization — the authorization only controls whether the restart proceeds with the intended reason code.

`src/infra/restart.ts` (+9/-0): Added a dynamic import of `server-reload-handlers.js` inside `resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart()`, calling `abortPendingChannelReloads()` with a best-effort catch. This is the belt-and-suspenders coverage: the restart state reset happens early in the in-process restart flow, before the SIGUSR1 handler in run-loop.ts gets a chance to fire its own abort call. The dynamic import pattern is used here (and in run-loop.ts) to avoid a static import dependency from the infra module into the gateway module, which would create a circular dependency.

**What did NOT change**: No changes to the channel reload start work flow itself — the `waitForActiveWorkBeforeChannelReload` poll logic (interval, timeout, still-pending warnings) is identical except for the abort generation checks. No changes to non-SIGUSR1 restart paths (config change reloads without a concurrent SIGUSR1 work identically to before). No changes to channel lifecycle outside the reload path. All existing tests pass including the updated stable runtime boundary test. The abort generation best-effort design means that even if the dynamic import fails or the generation check is somehow missed (e.g. the reload poll loop exits between two check points), channels will not be left in a broken state — the worst case is a redundant channel restart that gets EADDRINUSE and auto-retries as before this fix.

Fixes #94964

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Deferred channel reload cancellation on in-process restart (SIGUSR1). Prevents race between deferred channel reload and in-process restart that caused EADDRINUSE on Telegram webhook listener.

**Real environment tested**: Linux / OpenClaw Gateway with OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1 (in-process restart mode) / SIGUSR1 signal / Telegram and Feishu channels active with pending work

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1 ./openclaw gateway --config /etc/openclaw/config.yaml &
GATEWAY_PID=$!
echo $GATEWAY_PID > /tmp/gateway-94964.pid
sleep 10
kill -SIGUSR1 $GATEWAY_PID
sleep 30
cat /tmp/gateway-94964.ip.log
```

```bash
ps -p $GATEWAY_PID -o pid,state,etime
```

**After-fix evidence**:

Live Gateway runtime logs showing SIGUSR1 triggered in-process restart with deferred channel reload correctly cancelled:

```
2026-06-24T17:51:46.890+08:00 [gateway] loading configuration...
2026-06-24T17:51:53.922+08:00 [gateway] agent model: stepfun-plan/step-3.5-flash
2026-06-24T17:51:53.924+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (13 plugins; 6.9s)
2026-06-24T17:51:53.929+08:00 [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
2026-06-24T17:51:57.835+08:00 [gateway] ready
2026-06-24T17:52:36.808+08:00 [gateway] signal SIGUSR1 received
2026-06-24T17:52:36.813+08:00 [gateway] received SIGUSR1; restarting
2026-06-24T17:52:37.401+08:00 [feishu] feishu[default]: abort signal received, stopping
2026-06-24T17:52:37.446+08:00 [gateway] restart mode: in-process restart (OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN)
2026-06-24T17:52:37.451+08:00 [gateway] waiting for active work to drain before channel reload...
2026-06-24T17:52:37.451+08:00 [gateway] channel restart cancelled by in-process restart
2026-06-24T17:52:37.696+08:00 [gateway] loading configuration...
2026-06-24T17:52:37.701+08:00 [gateway] agent model: stepfun-plan/step-3.5-flash
2026-06-24T17:52:37.702+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (13 plugins; 0.1s)
2026-06-24T17:52:37.705+08:00 [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
2026-06-24T17:52:37.709+08:00 [gateway] ready
```

Key log events in sequence: (1) SIGUSR1 received at 17:52:36.808, (2) deferred reload poll loop starts waiting at 17:52:37.451, (3) Feishu channel receives abort, (4) applyHotReload detects the abort via generation check and logs "channel restart cancelled by in-process restart" at 17:52:37.451, (5) gateway reloads cleanly without EADDRINUSE or crash loop. The entire restart completes in under 1 second (17:52:36.808 to 17:52:37.709).

**Observed result after the fix**: SIGUSR1 triggers in-process restart. Deferred channel reload is correctly cancelled as shown by "channel restart cancelled by in-process restart" log message. No EADDRINUSE or crash loop occurs. The Telegram webhook listener starts exactly once under the new gateway lifecycle.

**What was not tested**: Fresh-start (non-reload) gateway initialization scenarios where no deferred channel reload is in progress — the generation counter is 0 by default and never checked in these paths. Concurrent SIGUSR1 signals arriving faster than 500ms poll interval — each signal independently triggers the abort path, and multiple calls to `abortPendingChannelReloads` are idempotent (setting the generation counter multiple times is harmless). Scenarios where the dynamic import itself fails (module not found, syntax error) — the best-effort catch silences the error and the deferred reload proceeds as before, which is the same behavior as pre-fix.

**Real evidence artifact links**: Runtime log transcript captured inline above from gateway stderr output during the test run.

## Risk checklist

- [x] **merge-risk**: Medium. Changes the restart lifecycle ordering; potential for missed abort if the dynamic import fails silently or the poll loop exits between check points. The abort is a best-effort signal — channels continue to function even if abort is missed. Three independent abort check points provide defense in depth. All test suites pass including the updated stable runtime boundary test.
- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [x] I have updated relevant documentation
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx proof.mjs` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity (`normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas`) through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 58 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: [AI-assisted] Preserve thread sessions across daily reset by default
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw should preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured thread sessions, while still giving operators an explicit way to keep topic/thread sessions continuous across daily boundaries.

There is also a closed-thread rollover bug for explicit idle/no-expiry thread policies: the Discord archived-thread helper previously marked sessions stale by zeroing `updatedAt`, but an idle/no-expiry policy has no idle deadline, so a later message can still reuse the closed thread session.

## Fix

- Preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured direct, group, and thread sessions.
- Document explicit topic/thread continuity with `session.resetByType.thread: { mode: "idle", idleMinutes: 0 }`.
- Add durable `sessionClosedAt` session-store state for closed thread sessions.
- Propagate `sessionClosedAt` through session freshness evaluation so closed sessions roll over even when idle expiry is disabled.
- Preserve the closed marker through system-event writes and clear it when a real user turn rolls into a fresh session.

## Evidence

Current-head proof after closed-marker consistency fix:

- **Current PR head tested:** `158d31748820`
- **Fix covered:** closed markers bypass provider-owned implicit-expiry skips, active provider-owned closed-thread runs defer rollover instead of archiving the active transcript, and the plugin runtime freshness helper sees `sessionClosedAt` as stale.
- **Runtime proof command:** `pnpm exec tsx --eval` against real `initSessionState()`, `replyRunRegistry`, and `resolveSessionEntryResetFreshness()`.
- **Focused acceptance command:** `CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/config/sessions/entry-freshness.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/thread-session-close.test.ts`
  - 3 Vitest shards passed
  - 194 tests passed
- **Whitespace check:** `git diff --check` passed

Terminal output from the current-head proof command:

```json
{
  "head": "158d31748820",
  "tempRoot": "openclaw-pr90226-proof-ZVmXug",
  "observations": {
    "pluginRuntimeFreshnessHelper": {
      "state": "stale",
      "fresh": false,
      "closedAt": 1768712399000
    },
    "providerOwnedActiveRunDeferral": {
      "isNewSession": false,
      "reusedPrevious": true,
      "previousSessionEntry": null,
      "sessionClosedAtPreserved": true,
      "archivedTranscriptCount": 0,
      "transcriptStillPresent": true
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result:

- Current head `158d31748820` reports closed provider-owned entries as stale through the plugin runtime freshness helper.
- Current head `158d31748820` keeps an active provider-owned closed-thread session on the existing `sessionId`, preserves `sessionClosedAt`, does not archive the active transcript, and leaves the transcript file in place while the active run is still running.

Validation:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`
  - 2 test files passed
  - 168 tests passed
- `git diff --check`
  - passed

Current-head follow-up validation after sibling-path fix:

- **Current PR head tested:** `05749da0ce`
- **Fix covered:** gateway and command-session rollover writers now clear `sessionClosedAt` when they mint a fresh `sessionId`, preventing repeated rollovers on the next turn.
- **Focused acceptance command:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`
  - 4 Vitest shards passed
  - 546 tests passed
- **Whitespace check:** `git diff --check` passed
- **Type/lint follow-up for current head:** `pnpm tsgo:prod` passed; `pnpm lint --threads=8` passed


Current-head real behavior proof after the sibling-path marker-clear follow-up:

- **Current PR head tested:** `05749da0ce`
- **Runtime path tested:** real `initSessionState()` from this PR branch via `pnpm exec tsx --eval`.
- **Environment/data:** local clean checkout with temporary `sessions.json` stores under `/tmp`; no live private transcripts or production identifiers.
- **What the proof covers:** final default-preserving behavior, explicit idle/no-expiry thread continuity, Discord closed-thread rollover via `sessionClosedAt`, and marker clearing after the current-head sibling-path fix.

Terminal output from the current-head proof command:

```json
{
  "head": "05749da0ce",
  "tempRoot": "openclaw-pr90226-proof-nCTMtC",
  "observations": {
    "unconfiguredThreadDailyDefault": {
      "previousSessionId": "daily-before-boundary",
      "sessionId": "2dc479df-d0b9-4260-9981-c6e633a40f7a",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "resetTriggered": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": false,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null
    },
    "explicitThreadIdleNoExpiry": {
      "previousSessionId": "explicit-idle-before-boundary",
      "sessionId": "explicit-idle-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": false,
      "reusedPrevious": true,
      "resetTriggered": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": false,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null
    },
    "closedDiscordThreadRollover": {
      "previousSessionId": "closed-discord-before-rollover",
      "sessionId": "c1cb048a-b723-404a-a37d-051e87749b29",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "resetTriggered": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": true,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null,
      "archivedTranscriptCreated": true,
      "activeTranscriptStillPresent": false
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result:

- Current head `05749da0ce` preserves implicit daily rollover for unconfigured thread sessions.
- Current head `05749da0ce` preserves explicit `resetByType.thread` idle/no-expiry continuity across the daily boundary.
- Current head `05749da0ce` rolls over a closed Discord thread session, archives the old transcript, removes the active old transcript, and clears `sessionClosedAt` on the new session entry.

Current-head real behavior proof before the sibling-path marker-clear follow-up:

- **Current PR head tested:** `f53724f2ca59`
- **Runtime path tested:** real `initSessionState()` from this PR branch via `pnpm exec tsx --eval`.
- **Environment/data:** local clean checkout with temporary `sessions.json` stores under `/tmp`; no live private transcripts or production identifiers.
- **What the proof covers:** final default-preserving behavior, explicit idle/no-expiry thread continuity, and Discord closed-thread rollover via `sessionClosedAt`.

Terminal output from the current-head proof command:

```json
{
  "head": "f53724f2ca59",
  "tempRoot": "openclaw-pr90226-proof-d7gIej",
  "observations": {
    "unconfiguredThreadDailyDefault": {
      "previousSessionId": "daily-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "explicitThreadIdleNoExpiry": {
      "previousSessionId": "explicit-idle-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": false,
      "reusedPrevious": true,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "closedDiscordThreadRollover": {
      "previousSessionId": "closed-discord-before-rollover",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": true,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null,
      "archivedTranscriptCreated": true,
      "activeTranscriptStillPresent": false
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result:

- Unconfigured thread sessions still roll over after the daily boundary, preserving shipped default behavior.
- Explicit `resetByType.thread` idle/no-expiry sessions keep the same `sessionId` across the daily boundary.
- A Discord thread session marked with `sessionClosedAt` creates a fresh session on the next user message, archives the old transcript, and clears the closed marker from the new session entry.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Discord or Telegram bot conversation in production. The proof uses the real local runtime session initialization path with redacted temporary session data.
