target.title: fix(agents): preserve compactionSummary in limitHistoryTurns (fixes #97590) (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a session has a configured `historyLimit` (or `dmHistoryLimit`) and has auto-compacted, `limitHistoryTurns` silently drops the leading `compactionSummary` message from the model context. The assistant loses all pre-compaction conversation context on every subsequent turn, even though compaction explicitly preserved a summary for that purpose.

## Why This Change Was Made

`limitHistoryTurns` counts `role === "user"` messages from the end and slices to the last N turns. When the retained tail exceeds the limit, the slice starts at a user message index > 0, discarding everything before it — including the `compactionSummary` at index 0 that `buildSessionContext` places there.

The fix: skip leading non-conversation messages (roles other than `"user"` / `"assistant"`, such as `compactionSummary` and `branchSummary`) before counting user turns, then prepend them back to the result.

## User Impact

Users with `historyLimit` or `dmHistoryLimit` configured will no longer lose summarized pre-compaction context. The assistant retains conversation continuity across compaction boundaries.

## Changes Made

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/history.ts`: `limitHistoryTurns` now preserves leading non-conversation messages (compactionSummary, branchSummary, etc.) when slicing by user turn count.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.limithistoryturns.test.ts`: Added 3 test cases covering compactionSummary preservation, branchSummary preservation, and non-conversation-only message arrays.

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed:** `limitHistoryTurns` drops the leading `compactionSummary` from model context when the session has a configured history limit and more user turns than the limit after compaction.
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, OpenClaw 2026.5.28, commit 19cac35a (upstream/main).
- **Steps run after the patch:** Invoked the production `limitHistoryTurns` function with a realistic post-compaction message array (compactionSummary + 3 user/assistant turn pairs) using `limit=2`.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ npx tsx -e 'import { limitHistoryTurns } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/history.ts"; ...'
Input: 7 messages, roles: ['compactionSummary','user','assistant','user','assistant','user','assistant']
After limit=2: 5 messages, roles: ['compactionSummary','user','assistant','user','assistant']
compactionSummary preserved: YES
User turns kept: 2
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The `compactionSummary` is preserved at index 0, the last 2 user turns and their assistant responses are retained, total 5 messages (was 4 before fix — summary was dropped).
- **What was not tested:** End-to-end session with real LLM provider (function is internal to embedded agent runner, not directly observable via CLI). The fix is verified through production function invocation and 12 unit tests (9 existing + 3 new) all passing.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `limitHistoryTurns` drops the leading `compactionSummary` from model context when the session has a configured history limit and more user turns than the limit after compaction.
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, OpenClaw 2026.5.28, commit 19cac35a (upstream/main).
- **Steps run after the patch:** Invoked the production `limitHistoryTurns` function with a realistic post-compaction message array (compactionSummary + 3 user/assistant turn pairs) using `limit=2`.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ npx tsx -e 'import { limitHistoryTurns } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/history.ts"; ...'
Input: 7 messages, roles: ['compactionSummary','user','assistant','user','assistant','user','assistant']
After limit=2: 5 messages, roles: ['compactionSummary','user','assistant','user','assistant']
compactionSummary preserved: YES
User turns kept: 2
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The `compactionSummary` is preserved at index 0, the last 2 user turns and their assistant responses are retained, total 5 messages (was 4 before fix — summary was dropped).
- **What was not tested:** End-to-end session with real LLM provider (function is internal to embedded agent runner, not directly observable via CLI). The fix is verified through production function invocation and 12 unit tests (9 existing + 3 new) all passing.

target.title: fix #96681: Duplicate reply: message-tool sends followed by short trailing acks ("已发 #xxx", "Sent above", "核心回答如下", "OK")
target.body:
Fixes #96681

## Summary

- Suppresses simple and compound post-message-tool meta acknowledgements such as `已发 #22141`, `Sent above`, `核心回答如下`, `OK`, `已发, 不再追加总结`, `Sent. Replied in thread.`, and `OK, that's the fix.` from final reply payloads after a same-route message-tool send.
- Keeps the existing long-text duplicate detector unchanged, including the 10-character floor and substring semantics.
- Preserves media payloads, route scoping, thread/account matching, provider configuration, and substantive short replies such as `OK, here is the actual answer.`.

## What Problem This Solves

When an agent sends the main response via the `message` tool, some models still append a short final acknowledgement. OpenClaw's delivery path treated that acknowledgement as a second source-channel reply, so users could see the main message followed by a duplicate-looking acknowledgement message.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A same-route message-tool send followed by text-only final payloads that are only simple or compound meta acknowledgements now resolves to no extra final delivery payload. Before this patch, the resolver returned those ack payloads as visible second replies because they were neither long enough for substring duplicate matching nor actual content duplicates of the sent body.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users no longer receive a second visible message that only acknowledges a message-tool send that already happened. This reduces duplicate-looking replies without suppressing distinct content replies, routed replies, or media replies.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The source-of-truth final payload path is `resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads` -> `filterMessagingToolDuplicates` -> `isMessagingToolDuplicate`. That duplicate helper intentionally ignores normalized text shorter than `MIN_DUPLICATE_TEXT_LENGTH = 10` and only checks overlap with `sentTexts`. Simple and compound post-tool-send acknowledgement text is usually below that floor or semantically unrelated to the long sent body, so the resolver returned it as a second payload.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix stays at the same source-of-truth resolver boundary where route-scoped message-tool sends are already filtered. It adds a conservative, anchored meta-ack invariant to the existing text-only duplicate filter instead of lowering the duplicate length floor or adding a downstream delivery workaround. That means the path that creates final source-channel payloads stops producing the extra ack payload in the first place.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the production resolver/runtime behavior covered by the regression and runtime log artifact. Full maintainer-side visible channel proof can still provide additional confidence, but the local artifact below exercises the resolver boundary that decides whether the trailing ack payload is delivered.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

Related open PR scan covered #96680 and #96763.

- #96763 adds a small ack matcher inside `filterMessagingToolDuplicates`, but its proof is a printed truth table rather than an in-repo resolver regression test, it marks broad phrases like `Thanks` as ack, and its commit metadata contains a disallowed attribution marker. This patch keeps the smaller integration point but adds RED/GREEN in-repo regression coverage, compound-ack coverage, and a narrower pattern set.
- #96680 adds a separate meta-commentary filter and wires it through multiple delivery paths. That is more comprehensive in shape but broader in diff surface. This patch instead folds the conservative meta-ack check into the existing source-of-truth text dedupe helper, so existing callers of `filterMessagingToolDuplicates` benefit without adding a second payload-filtering API.

## Real behavior proof

- **Real environment tested:** Linux worktree at `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97513`, exercising the production source-channel reply payload resolver and existing dedupe helper through the project's auto-reply reply Vitest config plus a standalone Node runtime proof outside the test runner. The runtime artifacts are embedded below with route details redacted where relevant.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts --reporter verbose
node --import tsx "$EVIDENCE_DIR/runtime-resolver-proof-20260629.mjs"
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
RED before initial implementation:
FAIL src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts > resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > drops short trailing meta acknowledgements after a same-route message tool send
expected [ { text: '已发 #22141' }, { text: 'Sent above' }, { text: '核心回答如下' }, { text: 'OK' } ] to strictly equal []

RED before compound-ack update:
FAIL src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts > resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > drops compound meta acknowledgements without dropping substantive short replies
expected only [{ text: "OK, here is the actual answer." }], but received compound ack payloads plus the substantive reply.

GREEN focused resolver tests after fix:
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  20 passed (20)

Related reply payload tests after fix:
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  45 passed (45)

Verbose runtime/log artifact after fix:
✓ resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > drops short trailing meta acknowledgements after a same-route message tool send
✓ resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > drops compound meta acknowledgements without dropping substantive short replies
✓ resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > dedupes duplicate replies when a messaging tool already sent to the same provider and target
✓ resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads > delivers distinct replies when a messaging tool already sent to the same provider and target

Standalone runtime resolver proof outside the test runner:
HEAD: e0f6690783ad66beae29f1981763085d8aa39891
Boundary: production resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads with same-route message-tool delivery metadata.
- same-route short final acknowledgement payloads -> []
- compound acknowledgements plus substantive short content -> [{ text: "OK, here is the actual answer." }]
- no prior message-tool sent text -> [{ text: "OK" }]
- different route -> [{ text: "Sent above" }]

Mantis Telegram Desktop dispatch attempt:
workflow_dispatch for PR #97513 returned HTTP 403: Must have admin rights to Repository.

Whitespace check:

passed with no output

Source test typecheck:
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo
passed with no output
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The simple-ack regression that previously returned four short ack payloads now returns `[]`; the compound-ack regression drops `已发, 不再追加总结`, `Sent. Replied in thread.`, and `OK, that's the fix.` while preserving `OK, here is the actual answer.` as a substantive short reply. The standalone runtime proof outside the test runner also shows ack-shaped text remains deliverable when there is no prior same-route message-tool sent text or when the message-tool send is for a different route. Existing route/media/text dedupe tests still pass.
- **What was not tested:** Full native Telegram Desktop/channel recording was not completed locally. A `workflow_dispatch` attempt for the Mantis Telegram Desktop proof on PR #97513 failed with `HTTP 403: Must have admin rights to Repository`, so an authorized maintainer can still add stronger visible-channel proof by dispatching the workflow or posting `@openclaw-mantis telegram desktop proof: verify a message-tool main reply followed by a short final ack shows one Telegram message and no trailing ack bubble.`

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts` covers the production resolver path; `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads.test.ts` was rerun to guard existing text/media/route dedupe helpers.
- **Scenario locked in:** A same-route `message` tool send records route text, then final payloads contain simple and compound acknowledgement text. The expected resolved payload list is empty for ack-only payloads, while `OK, here is the actual answer.` remains deliverable.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression sits at `resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads`, the same boundary that applies threading, route matching, media dedupe, and text dedupe before source-channel delivery. It catches the user-visible duplicate without requiring a brittle end-to-end fixture for every provider.

## Review findings addressed

- RF-001 / RF-003: Added an inspectable standalone runtime resolver proof outside the test runner, using the production `resolveFollowupDeliveryPayloads` boundary with same-route message-tool delivery metadata to show ack-only payloads resolve to no extra source-channel delivery payload.
- RF-002: The standalone runtime proof and regression test both preserve `OK, here is the actual answer.` as substantive short content; the standalone proof also preserves ack-shaped text when there is no prior same-route message-tool sent text and when the message-tool send targets another route.
- RF-004: Tried to trigger the maintainer-side Mantis Telegram Desktop proof for current PR head, but GitHub rejected `workflow_dispatch` with `HTTP 403: Must have admin rights to Repository`; the remaining native Telegram Desktop recording requires an authorized maintainer or repository actor. No additional code repair was identified by ClawSweeper.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk:message-delivery` and session-state-adjacent signals were considered because this patch suppresses final text payloads after message-tool sends and touches embedded-agent helper exports.
- **Risk explanation:** The patch quality warning is not a hidden default or fallback behavior change; the added `return false` is the normal non-match result for an anchored predicate. The public-surface-looking export is a helper barrel export used internally by reply payload dedupe, not a config/schema/protocol/default contract change.
- **Why acceptable:** The new predicate only runs after `sentTexts.length > 0`, inside the existing message-tool text dedupe filter, and media payloads return before the predicate. Legitimate long content, unrelated routes, payloads without prior sent text, and the explicit substantive short-reply guard keep the existing behavior.

## Risk / Compatibility

- **Patch quality notes:** The diff is limited to the existing dedupe helper and resolver tests. It adds a conservative predicate, wires it into the existing text dedupe helper, and covers both positive and false-positive cases. No unrelated formatting, lockfile, config, provider, or channel plugin changes are included.
- **What did NOT change:** The existing `MIN_DUPLICATE_TEXT_LENGTH` behavior, substring duplicate semantics, route matching, account/thread matching, media duplicate handling, provider configuration, and channel plugin behavior are unchanged.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical source-of-truth for final source-channel payload filtering remains `filterMessagingToolDuplicates` after `resolveMessagingToolPayloadDedupe` has chosen same-route sent text evidence. The fix does not add a second downstream delivery filter or alter provider routing contracts.

## What was not changed

- No API, bot setup, provider behavior, or channel plugin behavior was changed.
- No duplicate-length threshold was lowered.
- No fallback path was added for unknown messages or unknown routes.

target.title: model-usage: narrow parse_date except Exception to ValueError
target.body:
## Summary

- `skills/model-usage/scripts/model_usage.py`: `parse_date` wraps `datetime.strptime(...)` in a bare `except Exception`. The only thing `strptime` raises for a well-formed string input that doesn't match the format is `ValueError`. The broad `except Exception` also swallows `KeyboardInterrupt`/`SystemExit` (which subclass `BaseException`, not `Exception`, so this point is technically safe, but it also conceals unrelated bugs like a custom exception raised from a patched `datetime` class, an `ImportError` if the module is shadowed by something broken, or a `MemoryError` from a pathological input).

- The caller (`filter_by_days`) already guards against non-string `date` fields with `isinstance(day, str)` before calling `parse_date`, so a `TypeError` (raised when strptime receives a non-string) can't actually occur in this code path.

- Narrowed to `except ValueError` so the function only converts an unparseable date string to `None` — its documented behaviour — and any other unexpected error surfaces.

## Verification

- `parse_date("2024-01-01")` returns `date(2024, 1, 1)` (unchanged)
- `parse_date("not-a-date")` returns `None` (was None, still None, still catches the only thing it should)
- `parse_date(None)` would now propagate `TypeError` instead of being silently swallowed — which is what we want since the caller guards against this case anyway

target.title: fix #87407: [Bug]: retry on UND_ERR_SOCKET keep-alive failures
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Undici keep-alive pooled sockets can fail with `UND_ERR_SOCKET` on first use after being idle. This causes model requests to fail without retry, cascading through fallback models (#87407).
- Solution: add `isUndiciSocketError()` detector and one-retry logic in `buildGuardedModelFetch`. On `UND_ERR_SOCKET`, the fetch is retried once with a fresh TCP connection. The local service lease is properly released on retry failure.
- What changed: `provider-transport-fetch.ts` — new `isUndiciSocketError()` helper + retry in catch block. `provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` — 5 new tests.
- What did NOT change: no API, config, schema, or non-undici behavior changes. Only one retry attempt to avoid infinite loops.

## Maintainer checklist
- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in provider transport fetch
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — 83/83 tests pass including 5 new tests

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Undici keep-alive socket reuse failures (UND_ERR_SOCKET) cause model requests to fail without retry, cascading through fallback models.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/anthropic-undo-socket-87407 (01d31cec0)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  83 passed (83)
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- `isUndiciSocketError` correctly detects direct and nested UND_ERR_SOCKET errors ✓
- `isUndiciSocketError` returns false for non-socket, null, and different error codes ✓
- Existing buildGuardedModelFetch tests continue to pass ✓

**What was not tested:**
- Live undici connection pool with actual keep-alive timeout (requires network-level simulation)

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 58 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: refactor(terminal): optimize OSC progress label sanitization
target.body:
### Summary

Replaced the OSC progress label sanitizer's chained string passes with one module-level precompiled regex. The forbidden label tokens are still the same: OSC ST, BEL, C1 ST, bare ESC, and `]`.

### Benchmark

Environment: Node `v22.17.0` on native Windows, run from `C:\Users\user\Downloads\openclaw-pr`.

Method:
- compared the previous chained `replaceAll` / `split().join()` sanitizer against the new regex sanitizer
- verified old and new outputs matched for every benchmark input before timing
- used warmup plus 7 timing rounds per case and reported the median

Results:
- `plain-label` (26 chars): old `576.3 ns/op`, new `88.2 ns/op` -> `6.53x` faster (`-84.7%`)
- `short-osc` (11 chars): old `863.8 ns/op`, new `181.2 ns/op` -> `4.77x` faster (`-79.0%`)
- `mixed-controls` (20 chars): old `1274.2 ns/op`, new `333.5 ns/op` -> `3.82x` faster (`-73.8%`)
- `long-status` (2559 chars): old `23663.9 ns/op`, new `21921.4 ns/op` -> `1.08x` faster (`-7.4%`)

### Tests

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` completed. Note: emitted the existing engine warning because this local machine has Node `v22.17.0` while the repo asks for `>=22.19.0`.
- `pnpm test src/terminal/osc-progress.test.ts` passed: 1 test file, 2 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/terminal/osc-progress.ts src/terminal/osc-progress.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OSC progress labels are sanitized before being interpolated into terminal OSC 9;4 progress sequences.

Real environment tested: Native Windows checkout at `C:\Users\user\Downloads\openclaw-pr`, Node `v22.17.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the patched checkout, ran a local inline `node` benchmark script that invokes the old sanitizer and new sanitizer on representative OSC progress labels, asserts output equality before timing, then reports median nanoseconds per operation.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the patched checkout:

```text
$ node <inline osc-progress sanitizer benchmark script>
plain-label     length=  26 old=576.3 ns/op new=88.2 ns/op speedup=6.53x reduction=84.7%
short-osc       length=  11 old=863.8 ns/op new=181.2 ns/op speedup=4.77x reduction=79.0%
mixed-controls  length=  20 old=1274.2 ns/op new=333.5 ns/op speedup=3.82x reduction=73.8%
long-status     length=2559 old=23663.9 ns/op new=21921.4 ns/op speedup=1.08x reduction=7.4%
```

Observed result after fix: The benchmark completed after checking old/new output equality for plain, OSC-containing, mixed-control, and long repeated labels. The new sanitizer preserved the expected sanitized labels, including `Buildsafebaddone` for mixed OSC terminators/control characters, and measured faster across all benchmark cases.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Ghostty/WezTerm terminal session or the full repository test suite.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx proof.mjs` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity (`normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas`) through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

target.title: fix(gateway): run daily session resets on schedule
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users relying on configured daily session resets would see stale daily sessions persist past `atHour` until later inbound traffic touched the session-use reset path. During quiet periods, gateway uptime alone did not proactively roll those sessions over, so the configured daily reset contract was not enforced.

## Why This Change Was Made

This branch moves daily reset evaluation into gateway maintenance, scans the configured session stores on a timer, and calls the existing reset service only for rows that are actually stale past the configured daily boundary. It intentionally preserves active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions, and it reloads the current reset config on each sweep so policy changes take effect without a gateway restart.

## User Impact

Operators can now rely on daily session rollover happening during gateway uptime even when no new inbound message arrives after the reset boundary. Active runs stay bound, implicit provider-owned CLI sessions remain preserved under the default policy, and config changes to `session.reset`, `session.resetByType`, `session.resetByChannel`, or store targets are picked up on the next sweep.

## Evidence

Live source-checkout proof on the current branch exercised the real gateway maintenance timer against an isolated session store and showed the stale daily session rotating while the protected cases stayed intact:

```text
gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
[session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
gateway-proof: staleReset true
gateway-proof: activePreserved true
gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true
```

Focused follow-up validation on the same head also passed:

```text
$ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       18 passed (18)

$ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
exit_code=0
```

## Linked context

- Closes #82321
- Related PRs or discussions: none known beyond the review conversation on this PR.
- Maintainer or owner request: not separately requested; this addresses the filed daily session reset bug.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Daily sessions stale past `atHour` are reset by gateway maintenance without waiting for a new inbound session use, while active runs and implicit provider-owned CLI sessions are preserved.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-daily-session-reset`, running the patched gateway maintenance timer against an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. Bundled plugin facades were disabled for the proof process with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1` so the reset service did not depend on source-checkout plugin artifacts.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Ran a live Node command that starts `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` with the patched daily reset scheduler, current-config getter, real `performGatewaySessionReset` path, isolated temp session store, one stale daily session, one stale active-run session, and one implicit provider-owned CLI session.
  - Command shape: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... startGatewayMaintenanceTimers(...) ... TS`
  - Supplemental verification: `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts`
  - Supplemental type gates: `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```text
  $ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp> OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # creates isolated agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  # starts patched gateway maintenance timers
  # waits for daily reset sweep to invoke the real reset service
  TS
  gateway-proof: before {"stale":"old-regular-session","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  [session-daily-reset] daily session reset sweep complete
  gateway-proof: after {"stale":"5f959c12-f0ed-4ed1-99d2-cc158c66ac87","active":"old-active-session","cli":"old-cli-session"}
  gateway-proof: staleReset true
  gateway-proof: activePreserved true
  gateway-proof: implicitCliPreserved true

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       18 passed (18)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The running gateway maintenance timer reset the stale daily session through the actual reset service without any inbound message. The active-run session and implicit provider-owned CLI session kept their original session IDs. Focused coverage also verifies fresh sessions are left alone and later sweeps use current runtime config after reset policy changes.
- What was not tested: A multi-hour live gateway wait that naturally crosses the configured reset hour.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This proof exercises the real gateway maintenance timer and reset service in an isolated source-checkout runtime rather than a long-lived production gateway, so it validates the behavior safely without waiting for a natural multi-hour rollover.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Not captured as a separate live artifact on this head; before this fix, stale daily sessions could remain on their old session IDs past `atHour` until later inbound traffic hit the session-use reset path.

## Tests and validation

- Commands run:
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=<workspace>/.corepack pnpm test:channels`
  - `git diff --check` after resolving the current `upstream/main` merge on the last branch refresh
  - `git merge --no-edit upstream/main` from `2e6e17f7c50237bb0dd8a40414d29d27ee80a9d8`, then pushed refreshed head `f5aa20fd4aab098a9483fb3f89a09b9e672deb70`
- Regression coverage added or updated: `src/gateway/session-daily-reset-scheduler.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-hooks.test.ts`.
- What failed before this fix, if known? Daily reset staleness was only evaluated through session-use paths, so stale sessions could survive past `atHour` until later inbound traffic.
- If no test was added, why not? Not applicable; focused scheduler, maintenance, and reset-hook regression coverage was added or updated.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Configured daily resets now occur proactively during gateway uptime instead of waiting for the next message.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.
- What is the highest-risk area? Session-state compatibility for deployments that implicitly depended on lazy daily reset timing.
- How is that risk mitigated? The new behavior matches the configured wall-clock daily reset contract; the scheduler skips active runs, preserves implicit provider-owned CLI sessions under the default policy, avoids overlapping sweeps, and reloads current config before each sweep.

## Current review state

- What is the next action? Await fresh current-head CI and ClawSweeper re-review on head `538354adca8735efc71bc8d47936b273f647b350`.
- What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Fresh CI and reviewer confirmation are needed on the new head; no additional proof gap is open beyond the existing source-checkout real behavior proof already on this PR.
- Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? This head fixes the current ClawSweeper finding by preserving scheduled daily reset `session_end.reason === "daily"` in `performGatewaySessionReset` while keeping `before_reset` compatibility unchanged and adding focused reset-hook regression coverage.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 335ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

  1. Built OpenClaw from source on the PR branch

  6. Verified `cron list` and `cron show` returned the `lastRunWasManual` field without schema validation errors

  - `deleteAfterRun: true` — still set, NOT consumed by manual run ✅
  - `lastRunWasManual: true` — new field present and accepted by schema ✅
  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

<details>
<summary>Docker unit test output (82 tests pass)</summary>

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (57 tests) 254ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 44ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 25ms

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  82 passed (82)
   Start at  11:08:37
   Duration  2.77s (transform 1.83s, setup 250ms, import 2.09s, tests 323ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.85s
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>Docker Gateway CLI output</summary>

```
=== Node.js version ===
v24.16.0
=== Starting Gateway ===
[gateway] ready

TEST 1: Create deleteAfterRun at-job
  → id: dbfbe16b-c44a-4aa1-b820-889dbdbcb9a2, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000

TEST 2: Manual run on deleteAfterRun at-job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:dbfbe16b-..." }

TEST 3: Verify at-job survived manual run
  → id: dbfbe16b-..., enabled: true, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000 (PRESERVED)
  → status: "running" (agent executing but will error — no LLM provider in Docker)

TEST 4: Create recurring every-10s job
  → id: 18b2583a-7990-4667-95cd-591614c858f9, everyMs: 10000, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454

TEST 5: Manual run on recurring job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:18b2583a-..." }

TEST 6: Verify recurring job state after manual run
  → id: 18b2583a-..., enabled: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454 (PRESERVED, NOT pushed by backoff)

TEST 7: Schema validation — cron list --json
  → Both jobs returned with valid schema, no validation errors
  → total: 2, both enabled, both with preserved nextRunAtMs

Gateway stopped.
```

</details>

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

target.title: feat: add agent architecture runtime contracts
target.body:
## Summary
- Add runtime contracts for agent task state, tool results, compact summaries, memory judgment, and context fragments
- Add tests covering tool result bridge/model output/next hints, subagent task bridge, heartbeat task progress, and compaction helpers
- Document the agent architecture upgrade contract and integration points
- Merge latest `origin/main` into the fork branch and resolve the remaining approval-payload test conflict

## Test plan
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-result.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-result-bridge.test.ts src/agents/agent-tool-result-model-output.test.ts src/agents/agent-task-state.test.ts src/agents/agent-memory-judgment.test.ts`
  - unit-fast: 5 files passed, 118 tests passed
  - agents: `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts` passed, 34 tests passed
- `corepack pnpm check:test-types` was attempted locally after dependency install, but the process was killed before completion in this runner; no typecheck result is claimed here.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** External fork PR branch was conflicting with current `openclaw/openclaw:main`; the changed approval payload assertion helper now handles the current `presentation` response shape while preserving fallback support for older `interactive` / `channelData.execApproval` shapes.

target.title: fix(cli): add configure and onboard to interactive TTY command skip list
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw configure` and `openclaw onboard` hang and leave high-CPU child processes on some Linux kernel/Node.js combinations. Reported in #83560.

## Root Cause

The ExperimentalWarning suppression respawn in `buildCliRespawnPlan` wraps the command in a parent/child process pair sharing the same TTY fd. The parent installs signal handlers via `attachChildProcessBridge`, keeping the event loop alive. The child's clack `select()` calls `stdin.setRawMode(true)` on the shared fd. On Debian kernel 6.12.63 + Node 22.22.1 this causes the child to busy-wait at high CPU or hang indefinitely.

## Fix

Add `configure` and `onboard` to the existing `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS` set in `respawn-policy.ts`. This makes `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` return `true` for both, matching the existing treatment of `tui`, `terminal`, and `chat`. Because the function is driven by `resolveCliArgvInvocation(argv).primary` (via `getPrimaryCommand`), root options before the subcommand are already handled correctly — e.g. `openclaw --profile server configure` works without any additional argv-position logic.

`shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` is intentionally **not** changed: the CA-certs startup-environment respawn is safe for interactive commands because it only injects an env var without altering stdin/stdout or installing a signal bridge. Tests confirm `configure`/`onboard` still allow that respawn path.

## Changes

- `src/cli/respawn-policy.ts` (+1 line): add `"configure"` and `"onboard"` to `INTERACTIVE_TTY_COMMANDS`
- `src/cli/cli-utils.test.ts` (+23 lines): extend the existing `shouldSkipRespawnForArgv` test table with `configure`, `onboard`, and root-option variants; add a new describe block asserting `shouldSkipStartupEnvironmentRespawnForArgv` returns `false` for these commands

## Reviewer checklist

- Branch now based on upstream `main` @ `ff871e162a`
- Root-option invocations covered: `openclaw --profile server configure`, `openclaw --profile default onboard`
- CA-certs respawn behavior explicitly tested and unchanged
- No new argv-position parser introduced; existing `resolveCliArgvInvocation` does the work

## Acceptance criteria (for live repro)

```bash
timeout 12 script -qefc 'TERM=xterm-256color OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/oc-repro openclaw configure' /tmp/oc-repro.log
# Must exit cleanly; no high-CPU child processes remaining after exit
```

Fixes #83560

target.title: fix(discord): add native reply opt-out
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: Discord sends platform-native replies whenever a resolved `replyTo` value is present, but there was no Discord-scoped way for callers to explicitly opt out.
- Solution: Add a Discord-only `useReply` option for `message(action="send")`; `useReply: false` suppresses the native Discord reply reference.
- What changed: The Discord message tool schema now advertises `useReply`, and both Discord send action paths skip `replyTo` when it is false.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Shared message-tool threading, outbound reply inheritance, and non-Discord integrations are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Discord native replies can be useful by default, but some Discord workflows need responses to continue in the current channel or thread without attaching a native reply reference. Before this patch, callers could avoid an explicit `replyTo`, but they could not reliably opt out once a reply target had already been resolved by the surrounding message action flow.

This change keeps the default behavior intact while giving Discord callers a narrow escape hatch: set `useReply: false` to send a normal Discord message instead of a native reply.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord `message(action="send")` can now send without a native Discord reply when `useReply: false` is provided.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS with the Discord extension test suite, plus one live Discord send through the patched Discord action handler.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `npx --yes pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.ts extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.messaging.send.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts`
  - `npx --yes pnpm@11.1.0 exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Copied live output from a live Discord API fetch after sending message `1506101861780947064` with both `replyTo` and `useReply: false`:

    ```json
    {
      "ok": true,
      "sentMessageId": "1506101861780947064",
      "channelId": "1506080036107714610",
      "contentMatches": true,
      "hasMessageReference": false,
      "hasReferencedMessage": false,
      "messageReference": null,
      "referencedMessage": null
    }
    ```

  - Targeted Discord tests pass: 3 test files, 93 tests.
  - New tests assert that `message_reference` is omitted when `useReply: false` is supplied.
- Observed result after fix: The Discord send helpers preserve default reply behavior, but omit the native Discord reply target when `useReply` is false.
- What was not tested: A separately packaged release build was not tested; verification used the patched local checkout and live Discord API.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Existing Discord send paths always forwarded resolved `replyTo` into Discord `message_reference` when present.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord send handling treated any resolved `replyTo` value as an instruction to create a Discord native reply.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no Discord-level schema option or test case for explicitly suppressing native replies.
- Contributing context (if known): Reply target resolution can happen before Discord-specific handling, so the Discord adapter needs its own narrow opt-out.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `useReply: false` is exposed in the Discord message tool schema and prevents Discord `message_reference` from being sent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is isolated to Discord schema/action handling, so focused Discord tests cover the contract without changing shared message routing tests.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord `message(action="send")` now supports `useReply: false` as a Discord-only option. Defaults are unchanged: if `useReply` is omitted, Discord native replies still work as before.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
message(action="send", replyTo=...) -> Discord send -> native reply

After:
message(action="send", replyTo=...) -> Discord send -> native reply
message(action="send", replyTo=..., useReply=false) -> Discord send -> normal message
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 25.2.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.9.0, pnpm 11.1.0 via `npx --yes pnpm@11.1.0`
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord extension
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Inspect Discord message tool discovery and confirm `action="send"` includes `useReply`.
2. Send through the Discord action path with `replyTo` and default options.
3. Send through the Discord action path with `replyTo` and `useReply: false`.
4. Run the targeted Discord tests.

### Expected

- Default Discord sends keep using native replies when `replyTo` is present.
- Discord sends with `useReply: false` omit the native reply reference.
- Non-Discord message behavior is unchanged.

### Actual

- A live Discord send with both `replyTo` and `useReply: false` created message `1506101861780947064`; reading it back from Discord returned no `message_reference` and no `referenced_message`.
- Targeted tests confirm default reply behavior is preserved.
- Targeted tests confirm `message_reference` is omitted when `useReply: false` is supplied.
- The diff is limited to Discord extension files.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Live Discord verification:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "sentMessageId": "1506101861780947064",
  "channelId": "1506080036107714610",
  "contentMatches": true,
  "hasMessageReference": false,
  "hasReferencedMessage": false,
  "messageReference": null,
  "referencedMessage": null
}
```

Targeted verification:

```text
npx --yes pnpm@11.1.0 exec vitest run \
  extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts

Test files: 3 passed
Tests: 93 passed
```

Formatting:

```text
npx --yes pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 \
  extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.messaging.send.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `useReply` is exposed only through the Discord message tool schema contribution.
  - Default Discord `replyTo` behavior is preserved.
  - `useReply: false` suppresses Discord `message_reference` in both Discord send paths.
  - A live Discord send with `replyTo` and `useReply: false` produced no native Discord reply metadata when fetched back from Discord.
  - The diff touches only Discord extension files.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `useReply` omitted behaves like the previous default.
  - `useReply: false` with a present `replyTo` does not forward a native reply reference.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - A separately packaged release build; verification used the patched local checkout and live Discord API.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Callers may assume `useReply` applies to all integrations.
  - Mitigation: The schema description explicitly marks it Discord-only, and the implementation is limited to the Discord extension.
- Risk: Default Discord reply behavior could regress.
  - Mitigation: Tests cover default reply behavior and the explicit opt-out path separately.
